name: Ethical Valence Theory
title: EF-05 — Ethical Valence Theory (EVT)
foundation: AV decisions must minimise expected harm AND respect socially derived valence hierarchies that reflect public moral preferences about which road users deserve priority. Combines consequentialist harm calculation with a contractualist social weighting layer derived from empirical studies of public moral intuitions. The moral profile of the AV is a transparent, configurable parameter rather than a hidden design choice.
decision_logic: Step 1: Compute expected harm for each action using the delta-v based injury model: H_i = k * delta_v_i^2 where k is a structural vulnerability constant for the road user type. Step 2: Rank candidate actions using the valence hierarchy for the active moral profile. Altruistic profile: pedestrians rank above passengers, children above adults. Threshold-egoistic profile: protect the passenger unless the harm to third parties exceeds a configurable threshold T. Step 3: Select the action that minimises expected harm within the valence constraints. The MDP formulation allows multi-step look-ahead over short time horizons.
pros: Integrates empirical public moral preferences rather than imposing a single philosophical view
Configurable moral profiles make the ethical stance of the AV explicit and auditable
Formally specified within MDP framework making it computationally tractable
Explicitly treats social acceptability as a first-class design criterion alongside safety
Allows manufacturers to offer different moral profiles with transparent disclosure
cons: Valence criteria may embed demographic discrimination by age, gender, or social status
Moral profiles must be publicly disclosed creating political and legal sensitivity
Short temporal horizon in MDP formulation may miss downstream consequences
The correct moral profile is contested — no objective criterion for choosing one
Empirical public preferences may not align with philosophical or legal ethical standards
best_fit_scenarios: unavoidable crash dilemmas with clearly distinct stakeholder categories such as passenger vs pedestrian
deployments requiring explicit public justification of the moral stance taken
research contexts exploring sensitivity to different moral profiles
scenarios where the social identity of road users is distinguishable and legally relevant
jurisdictions requiring manufacturers to disclose the ethical principles of their AV systems
poor_fit_scenarios: routine non-dilemma driving where valence hierarchy is irrelevant
contexts where demographic weighting creates legal discrimination liability
scenarios requiring fast real-time decisions where MDP look-ahead is too slow
use_when: collision_unavoidable is true AND ego_vehicle.passenger_at_risk is true in the scenario alongside at least one VRU stakeholder — the passenger is a scored stakeholder and the dilemma has distinct social categories
collision_unavoidable is true AND the dilemma involves clearly distinct stakeholder categories such as passenger vs pedestrian
the social identity or role of road users (child, pedestrian, passenger) is a morally relevant distinguishing factor between available actions
the deployment context requires explicit disclosure of the moral profile used
avoid_when: collision_unavoidable is false — routine driving does not require valence hierarchy reasoning
stakeholder types are not distinguishable or all parties belong to the same category
demographic weighting would create legal discrimination liability in the deployment jurisdiction
dominant_when: collision_unavoidable is true AND ego_vehicle.passenger_at_risk is true in the scenario AND at least one VRU stakeholder (pedestrian, child, cyclist) also appears in risk_score_matrix — the co-presence of passenger and VRU as scored stakeholders defines a passenger-vs-VRU dilemma and makes EF-05 dominant

collision_unavoidable is true AND the action that best protects the passenger differs from the action that best protects the VRU — the cross-category trade-off between passenger and pedestrian is the axis that determined the selected action
tradeoffs: vs Utilitarian (EF-01): EF-05 adds a social valence layer on top of harm minimization — EF-01 minimises raw aggregate harm without considering the social identity of those harmed. vs Maximin (EF-03): EF-05 is not purely worst-case — it balances valence hierarchy with harm magnitude, whereas EF-03 focuses only on risk magnitude. vs Ethics of Risk (EF-04): EF-04 uses objective risk metrics; EF-05 adds subjective social weighting — they are addressing different ethical dimensions and can be combined. vs Deontological (EF-02): EVT is outcome-focused; EF-02 is rule-based — EVT would choose to violate a rule if the valence-weighted outcome justifies it.
key_parameters: delta_v impact velocity change per road user
structural_vulnerability_constant k per road user type
valence_rank V(road_user_type) from active moral profile
source_papers: Evans et al. (2020) — Ethical Decision Making in Autonomous Vehicles: The AV Ethics Project
